output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does scoring a raw-payload-byte transformer malware detector with a single accuracy or F1 computed on a held-out split of the same capture and testbed conflate genuine malicious-payload semantic detection skill with testbed artifacts - environment byte fingerprints, synthetic-attack-generator signatures, and near-duplicate payload leakage from exact-deduplication-only preprocessing - so that the score must be promoted to an artifact-marginalized, intervention-resolved detection profile that separates semantic-attributable from artifact-attributable accuracy via controlled byte-level interventions and cross-testbed transfer, reports their interaction as a third labeled component, and provably collapses back to the reported scalar under the same-testbed split with no intervention?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Raw-payload-byte transformers are being pitched as drop-in production intrusion and malware detectors: feed the model the bytes of a TCP or UDP payload, read off a binary malicious/benign verdict and a multi-class attack type, and deploy. The operational stakes ride entirely on what the reported accuracy or F1 actually measures. A single scalar computed on a held-out split of the same capture and testbed silently conflates three very different things: genuine malicious-payload semantic skill, and artifact signal from (a) environment byte fingerprints (MAC/IP/host strings, application banners, TLS/SNI fields, capture-tool quirks that survive into the raw bytes), (b) synthetic-attack-generator signatures (UNSW-NB15 is IXIA PerfectStorm tool-generated; CIC-IoT23 is a fixed device lab), and (c) near-duplicate payload leakage, because preprocessing that drops only EXACT hex duplicates lets attack variants leak across train and test. When most of a headline number is artifact, the deployment story inverts into a concrete evasion surface: an attacker does not need to change malicious semantics at all, only the testbed-specific bytes the model secretly keyed on - rewrite a banner, perturb a header field, alter a host string - and the detector that scored 0.99 in the paper collapses in the wild. This is exactly the sampling-bias and spurious-correlation failure mode that Arp et al. (USENIX Security 2022) warn about, made concrete for raw-byte transformers: the model can be right on the test set for reasons that have nothing to do with the threat it claims to detect, and both heads (binary and attack-type) are exposed, since the attack-type head can route on generator fingerprints just as easily as the binary head routes on environment bytes.

The scientific-integrity stakes for the payload-byte-model subfield are equally serious and more systemic. Sommer and Paxson (IEEE S&P 2010) argued more than a decade ago that closed-world, same-environment evaluation overstates what a network anomaly detector has actually learned; TESSERACT (Pendlebury et al., USENIX Security 2019) made the analogous point quantitatively for the temporal axis. The payload-transformer literature inherited the opposite habit: a leaderboard of same-testbed scalars on a small set of canonical captures. If a large fraction of each number is testbed artifact, then every entry on those leaderboards is contaminated in a way the scalar cannot reveal, and architecture-versus-architecture comparisons may be measuring which model best memorizes IXIA PerfectStorm and the CIC device lab rather than which model best detects malice. Without a decomposition that separates semantic-attributable from artifact-attributable accuracy, the field cannot tell genuine progress from progress at fitting the capture, and published improvements are not safely interpretable as detection skill.

The reason this question is worth answering, rather than merely lamenting, is that it yields a durable, reusable measurement-hygiene contribution rather than a one-off audit. The deliverable is an artifact-marginalized, intervention-resolved detection profile that additively splits a detector's accuracy into a semantic-attributable component, an artifact-attributable component, and an explicit third interaction component - turning a single opaque number into a diagnostic. Concretely it produces three artifacts the subfield currently lacks: a per-deployment trust certificate for defenders (a measurement result, not a deployment-readiness certification) that states which attack families and which testbeds a detector's skill survives marginalization (so a SOC knows where the model is trustworthy and where its score was borrowed from the capture); a developer diagnostic that says how much of the headline accuracy is artifact-limited versus how much is genuine semantic headroom still on the table; and reproducible cross-testbed leaderboards (across UNSW-NB15, CIC-IoT23, CIC-IDS2017, TON_IoT, CTU-13) that rank detectors by transferable skill instead of capture-fit. Crucially, the profile is built to be strict-generalizing: under the same-testbed split with no intervention it provably collapses back to the paper's published scalar (the B1 reduction). That means the field can adopt the richer profile without discarding any existing result - the old number is recovered exactly as one corner of the new measurement, so the upgrade is additive, not a repudiation, which is precisely what makes a measurement-hygiene template adoptable at scale.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The core difficulty is a MEASUREMENT problem, not engineering effort: the paper's same-testbed held-out scalar (accuracy / F1 / macro-F1) is structurally unable to separate SEMANTIC malicious-payload skill from TESTBED-ARTIFACT skill, because both are present in every held-out sample drawn from the same capture environment, the same synthetic attack generator, and the same near-duplicate-laden payload pool. A single number computed on that split is mathematically a sum of the two contributions plus their interaction; no amount of additional same-testbed evaluation can decompose a sum into its addends. The obstacles below make that non-identifiability concrete.

1. Attribution non-identifiability from one scalar. The obstacle: one held-out accuracy value confounds three quantities at once - semantic-attributable accuracy, artifact-attributable accuracy, and their interaction - and there is no semantic ground-truth label that says "this byte was malicious because of attack content" versus "this byte was predictive because it is an IXIA PerfectStorm generator fingerprint or a lab MAC/IP/SNI string." Why naive fails: simply reporting more same-testbed metrics (add precision, recall, ROC-AUC, per-class F1) adds digits but not degrees of freedom - every one of them is computed on the same artifact-contaminated split and inherits the same confound, so they all move together and reveal nothing. Why tractable: the decomposition is recoverable with TWO orthogonal instruments that the scalar lacks - (a) controlled byte-level interventions that destroy semantic structure while preserving artifact structure (byte-order shuffle keeps the byte histogram but kills n-gram/order semantics; environment-field masking zeroes byte ranges carrying MAC/IP/host/SNI/app-banner strings), and (b) a cross-testbed transfer oracle (train UNSW-NB15 brute-force, test CIC-IoT23 brute-force) that strips testbed-specific signal. The PRIMARY artifact estimate comes from instrument (a): accuracy that survives byte-order shuffle and environment-field masking is artifact-attributable with no domain shift involved, pinned by the synthetic-injection calibration anchor; the same-testbed-minus-cross-testbed gap (B1 - B4) is a CORROBORATING UPPER BOUND that also absorbs ordinary distribution shift (different attack tooling, different benign mix), so it bounds rather than equals artifact inflation. The interaction is an explicit third estimated term, never folded into a main effect.

2. Localizing artifact byte-ranges without header access. The obstacle: to mask environment fingerprints we must first locate WHICH payload byte ranges carry them, but the model ingests raw payload bytes with no header/field schema, and artifact bytes (an embedded SNI host string, an app banner, a tool-injected timestamp) sit inline among semantic bytes. Why naive fails: trusting the transformer's own attention weights as the artifact map is unfaithful - attention is well documented to be a poor causal explanation (it can be high on tokens that do not change the output), so masking "high-attention" bytes can remove semantic content and leave artifacts, biasing the estimate in an unknown direction. Why tractable: use CAUSAL byte-occlusion sensitivity instead of attention - systematically zero/permute contiguous byte windows and measure the actual output change (a leave-one-window-out occlusion map), cross-checked against a documented byte-range LOCATOR that finds high-entropy printable-string runs and known protocol-field offsets (SNI in TLS ClientHello, HTTP Host header, banner regexes). Occlusion is faithful by construction because it intervenes on the input and reads the realized prediction delta; the locator gives an independent, header-free prior so the two can be agreement-checked.

3. Near-duplicate leakage measurement plus cross-testbed taxonomy harmonization. The obstacle: the paper drops only EXACT-duplicate payloads, so near-duplicates (same attack, one byte differs - a re-randomized session id, an incremented sequence number) leak across the train/test split and inflate the scalar; separately, transfer requires aligning attack families whose labels differ across corpora (UNSW-NB15 "Exploits/Fuzzers/Generic" vs CIC-IoT23 device-flood/DDoS families vs CIC-IDS2017 vs TON_IoT vs CTU-13 botnet labels). Why naive fails: exact-hash dedup misses near-duplicates entirely (one flipped byte = different hash), so the leakage is invisible to the paper's own pipeline; and naive label-string matching across testbeds either merges semantically different attacks or discards most data, collapsing the transfer grid. Why tractable: MinHash/SimHash over hex payloads (Broder 1997) gives scalable approximate near-duplicate detection at a chosen Jaccard threshold, yielding a quantified near-duplicate leakage RATE that the paper inherits and a near-dedup retrain to measure its effect; taxonomy harmonization is handled by an explicit, published mapping to a small set of shared families (brute-force, DoS/DDoS-flood, port/recon scan, botnet C2, web/injection) with UNAMBIGUOUS cells evaluated and ambiguous cells reported as uncovered (per the coverage rule) rather than force-mapped.

4. Benign-balance confound and statistical multiplicity. The obstacle: the paper balances classes by RANDOM benign subsampling, which can itself inject a benign artifact (the retained benign sample may over-represent a particular host/app whose byte fingerprint becomes a spurious "benign" signal), and we will evaluate across a large grid of testbed x attack-family x head (binary and attack-type) combinations, inviting false discoveries. Why naive fails: a single random benign subsample plus uncorrected per-cell significance tests will surface artifact fractions that are sampling-flukes; "it was significant in one cell" is meaningless across dozens of cells. Why tractable: repeat the benign subsampling under multiple seeds and a stratified (per-host/per-app) alternative to bound the balance-induced variance, and control the testbed x family x head grid with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR so the reported artifact fractions survive multiplicity; both heads are carried through every cell so binary and attack-type confounds are estimated separately.

5. Interaction estimation under broken i.i.d. assumptions. The obstacle: we need a clean ADDITIVE estimate of semantic + artifact + interaction, but network payloads violate the independence that bootstrap confidence intervals assume - duplicate clusters and flow/temporal autocorrelation mean the effective sample size is far below the row count, and the semantic x artifact interaction is precisely the term most sensitive to that over-counting. Why naive fails: a vanilla i.i.d. row-level bootstrap treats 10,000 near-duplicate payloads from one attack burst as 10,000 independent draws, producing absurdly tight intervals that will "confirm" any interaction term; standard random k-fold also leaks duplicates across folds. Why tractable: use a BLOCK/CLUSTER bootstrap that resamples whole duplicate clusters (from the MinHash step) and space-time blocks as units, apply an effective-sample-size correction, and report the interaction with intervals computed on those clusters - the same temporal/spatial-bias discipline established for malware evaluation by TESSERACT (Pendlebury et al., USENIX Security 2019). This makes the additive decomposition honest about how much independent evidence actually exists.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The problem sits in the unclaimed seam between two communities that have never crossed the attribution axis. On one side is the payload- and packet-byte representation-learning community - nPrint and nPrintML (Holland et al., CCS 2021), ET-BERT (Lin et al., WWW 2022), and the broader line of byte-level packet transformers and raw-payload models - which builds the architectures and reports same-testbed held-out scalars as the measure of success. On the other side is the security-ML methodology-critique community - Arp et al. (USENIX Security 2022), Sommer and Paxson (IEEE S&P 2010), and TESSERACT (Pendlebury et al., USENIX Security 2019) - which knows the pitfalls of sampling bias, spurious correlation, and biased evaluation, but has documented them largely qualitatively and at the flow or feature level, and has not built a constructive artifact-versus-semantic variance attribution for raw-byte payload transformers specifically. The model-builders own the architecture but not the critique; the critics own the critique but have not instrumented this architecture. The cross-product of the two - a byte-level, testbed-marginalized decomposition of a payload transformer's score into semantic, artifact, and interaction components - is the quadrant neither side has claimed, and that is precisely why it remains open.

Several structural barriers kept it open. Model-builders are optimized against the same-testbed scalar and have neither the incentive nor the tooling to attribute their own accuracy to byte-level artifacts; a decomposition that subtracts artifact credit from a headline number is professionally unrewarded under leaderboard norms, and the byte-level interventions needed to do it (masking environment fields, ablating generator signatures, near-duplicate-aware deduplication) are not part of any standard pipeline. The critics, conversely, stop at diagnosis: they show that leakage and bias exist but do not deliver a constructive estimator that says how much of a given detector's accuracy is genuine. Two further frictions reinforce the gap. Cross-testbed transfer - the natural way to expose artifact reliance - is genuinely hard because attack taxonomies, label granularities, and capture conventions differ across UNSW-NB15, CIC-IoT23, CIC-IDS2017, TON_IoT, and CTU-13, so aligning an attack family across testbeds is real work and most authors simply avoid it and stay in-distribution. And exact-duplicate-only deduplication is the inherited default: it is what the reference preprocessing does, it looks principled, and it quietly permits near-duplicate variants to leak across the split, so the leakage channel is baked into the standard recipe and nobody is forced to confront it.

This approach is different because it is constructive, provable, and byte-level, rather than diagnostic and feature-level. Instead of asserting that leakage inflates scores, it builds the marginalized profile and resolves the inflation with controlled interventions: a byte-order-shuffle plus environment-field-masking placebo that kills semantics while preserving byte-frequency artifacts, paired with a cross-testbed swap that kills artifacts while preserving semantics, and the two must dissociate the scalar from the semantic measure (if they move together, or there is no cross-testbed drop, the framework is falsified). It is provably anchored: the B1 corner reproduces the paper's exact scalar, so the decomposition extends the published result rather than contradicting it, which is the property prior critiques never offered. And it is testbed-marginalized at the level where the artifacts physically live - the raw bytes - which distinguishes it sharply from its siblings: TESSERACT addresses temporal bias (a static split overstating skill as time passes), while generic dataset-bias work operates on flows and engineered features. Here the unit of intervention is the payload byte, the marginalization is over testbeds, and the target is the specific payload-transformer two-head design (binary plus attack-type), so artifact inflation is measured as the gap between the same-testbed scalar and a cross-testbed transfer oracle on a matched attack family, with the semantic-by-artifact interaction reported as its own labeled component rather than folded into either main effect.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

The approach is a runnable pipeline that promotes the paper's same-testbed scalar to an artifact-marginalized, intervention-resolved detection PROFILE that separates semantic- from artifact-attributable accuracy, reports their interaction as a labeled third component, and provably collapses to the reported scalar under a same-testbed split with no intervention (B1).

1. Data and harmonized cross-testbed taxonomy. Keep the paper's UNSW-NB15 (IXIA PerfectStorm generator) and CIC-IoT23 (105-device lab); ADD CIC-IDS2017, TON_IoT, and CTU-13 for transfer; optionally ISCX-VPN / an encrypted corpus for an encrypted-payload blind-spot extension [to verify availability]. Preprocessing mirrors the paper (payload-bearing TCP/UDP only, hex->decimal byte input) so B1 is exact. A published harmonization table maps native labels onto a shared family set - brute-force, DoS/DDoS-flood, recon/port-scan, botnet C2, web/injection, plus benign - and only UNAMBIGUOUS family cells are scored; ambiguous mappings are reported as uncovered.

2. Controlled byte-level intervention battery as a CALIBRATION harness (synthetic-injection anchor). The disentangling instrument is a battery of input interventions, not a new model: (a) byte-order shuffle (preserves byte histogram, destroys order/n-gram semantics); (b) environment-field masking that zeroes byte ranges located by a documented header-free LOCATOR (high-entropy printable-string runs, SNI/Host/banner offsets) and confirmed by causal byte-occlusion maps; (c) synthetic-generator-signature ablation (remove or randomize generator-constant byte spans). Because real semantic ground truth is unavailable, the battery is CALIBRATED against KNOWN-magnitude injected artifacts (per team pattern d): plant byte fingerprints of controlled effect size into a copy of the data, then verify the estimator recovers the injected artifact fraction. This makes the attribution estimator itself testable.

3. Near-duplicate dedup and leakage rate. Replace exact-dedup-only with MinHash/SimHash near-duplicate detection over hex payloads (Broder 1997) at a reported Jaccard threshold; report the near-duplicate LEAKAGE RATE the paper inherits and retrain under near-dedup to quantify its contribution to the scalar.

4. Additive intervention/variance-components estimator with the B1-B4 collapse property. The estimator produces three labeled terms - SEMANTIC-attributable, ARTIFACT-attributable, and INTERACTION - via the boundary ladder: B1 (same-testbed random split, no intervention, identity byte->decimal mapping) reproduces the paper's accuracy/F1 EXACTLY, pinned by a unit-test harness against the published number; B2 (placebo: byte-order shuffle + environment-field masking) confirms the confound if the same-testbed scalar MOVES while the cross-testbed semantic measure does not; B3 marginalizes across all five testbeds with near-duplicate dedup; B4 (cross-testbed transfer oracle, train A test B same family) gives the semantic floor, so ARTIFACT inflation is estimated PRIMARILY from the within-testbed intervention battery and the byte-histogram probe (both domain-shift-free) calibrated by synthetic injection, while B1 - B4 is reported as a domain-shift-inclusive UPPER BOUND that corroborates direction and magnitude; the interaction is an explicit third component, never folded into a main effect. The whole profile collapses to the reported scalar under B1 with no intervention, by construction.

5. Baselines and the full ablation/transfer matrix. Baselines: the paper's transformer; nPrintML (Holland et al., CCS 2021); ET-BERT (Lin et al., WWW 2022); a byte-HISTOGRAM / byte-frequency logistic regression (the artifact-sufficiency probe); and a bag-of-byte-n-grams model. Ablations: byte-order shuffle, environment-field masking, exact-dedup vs near-dedup retrain, generator-signature removal, and the full cross-testbed transfer matrix (every train-testbed x test-testbed x shared-family cell). Both heads (binary AND attack-type) are evaluated in every cell. The transfer matrix requires per-train-testbed model training beyond the near-dedup retrain, which is feasible because the payload-byte transformer and baselines are small models on small captures, keeping the study runnable.

6. Metrics and statistics. Report accuracy + macro-F1 (B1 reproduction), cross-testbed retained accuracy (the semantic measure), artifact-attributable fraction (within-testbed intervention estimate; B1 - B4 reported alongside as a domain-shift-inclusive upper bound), near-duplicate leakage rate, and byte-occlusion sensitivity maps - per head. Confidence intervals use a BLOCK/CLUSTER bootstrap that resamples whole duplicate clusters (from MinHash) and space-time blocks, with an effective-sample-size correction; significance across the testbed x family x head grid is controlled with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR. This follows the temporal/spatial-bias discipline of TESSERACT (Pendlebury et al., USENIX Security 2019).

7. Expected outcomes as falsifiable predictions. (i) The artifact-attributable fraction - estimated within-testbed from the intervention battery and byte-histogram probe (calibrated by synthetic injection), with B1 - B4 as a corroborating domain-shift-inclusive upper bound - is greater than 0 and statistically significant - FALSIFIED if the same-testbed scalar and the cross-testbed semantic measure move together (artifact fraction indistinguishable from 0) or there is no statistically significant cross-testbed drop. (ii) The byte-histogram logistic baseline MATCHES the transformer same-testbed but COLLAPSES cross-testbed - a positive corroborator proving the same-testbed number is artifact-dominated. (iii) The semantic measure stays STABLE under artifact removal (masking, near-dedup, generator-signature ablation). DO-NO-HARM check: in a genuinely semantic regime the verdict must be preserved - a model that truly learned malicious-payload content must SURVIVE artifact removal AND TRANSFER cross-testbed, so the method must not penalize a legitimately semantic detector. Failure to transfer cross-testbed is NOT by itself proof of artifact reliance, because genuine cross-testbed semantic divergence (different attack tooling actually changes malicious content) also depresses transfer; the within-testbed semantic-destroying interventions are the discriminator - only if accuracy survives them is the same-testbed score artifact-attributable. All strong claims are confined to well-covered testbed/family/head cells, and coverage fraction is reported alongside every artifact-fraction estimate (per team pattern j).

**Limitations and Responsible-AI Notes**

This is a defensive, measurement-hygiene method: it audits how payload-byte detectors are EVALUATED and does not build, improve, or release any malware, payloads, or evasion tooling - the byte-order-shuffle and environment-field-masking interventions are evaluation controls applied to the detector's own test inputs, not attack recipes, and no perturbed traffic is emitted toward any system. The interventions assume cleartext transport-layer payloads, so the cleartext-byte analysis and several artifact channels may not extend to TLS/QUIC-encrypted traffic that dominates real networks; we treat the encrypted-payload setting as an explicit out-of-scope limitation rather than a solved case. All experiments rely solely on public, already-released benchmark datasets (UNSW-NB15, CIC-IoT23, CIC-IDS2017, TON_IoT, CTU-13), introducing no new captures of real user traffic. Finally, artifact-fraction and semantic-attribution claims are confined to well-covered testbed-by-family-by-head cells with coverage fraction reported alongside every estimate, and are not asserted for sparse or unharmonizable cells, so the method audits the leaderboard without itself overclaiming where the evidence is thin.
